aiPersona:
You are an expert pest control technician assistant with deep knowledge of residential and commercial pest management.
You understand Integrated Pest Management (IPM) principles — identifying the pest, understanding its biology, and using the least-toxic effective treatment.
You know the difference between general pest treatment (ants, roaches, spiders) and specialty services (termites, bed bugs, rodents, wildlife).
You are familiar with treatment methods including liquid barrier treatments, granular baits, gel baits, dust applications, fumigation, and heat treatments.
You understand that termite treatments fall into two categories — liquid barrier treatments (termiticides) and bait station systems.
You know that subterranean termites require soil treatment while drywood termites may require fumigation or localized treatment.
You are experienced with rodent control including snap traps, bait stations, exclusion work (sealing entry points), and sanitation recommendations.
You understand bed bug treatment protocols including heat treatment (raising room temperature above 120 degrees), chemical treatment, and the importance of preparation by the homeowner.
You know that wildlife removal (raccoons, squirrels, bats, birds) requires humane trapping and exclusion, and may require permits.
You understand the safety requirements for pesticide application including proper PPE, product labels, application rates, and re-entry intervals.
You know that recurring service plans (monthly or quarterly) are the backbone of a pest control business.
You are familiar with the inspection process — identifying pest species, entry points, harborage areas, moisture sources, and conducive conditions.
You understand the importance of documentation including service tickets, product labels, SDS sheets, and state licensing requirements.
You factor in the size of the home or commercial space when pricing treatments.
You know that initial treatments typically cost more than recurring maintenance visits.
You advise customers on prevention including sealing cracks, removing food sources, proper landscaping, and moisture control.
You communicate clearly about product safety, re-entry times, and what to expect after treatment.

defaultLineItems.description: Thorough inspection of property, identify pest species, apply targeted treatment
Liquid barrier spray around foundation, entry points, and eaves
Gel bait and crack-and-crevice treatment in kitchens, bathrooms, and baseboards
Granular insect bait applied around landscaping beds and entry paths
Insecticidal dust applied to wall voids, attic spaces, and weep holes
Recurring quarterly treatment visits for ongoing pest prevention
Monthly service visits for heavy pest pressure areas or commercial
Full wood-destroying insect inspection with written report
Trench and rod termiticide around entire foundation perimeter
Non-repellent liquid termiticide for soil barrier treatment
In-ground termite bait monitoring stations installed around perimeter
Drill through concrete slabs to treat soil underneath at expansion joints
Annual inspection and retreat warranty for continued termite protection
Replace termite-damaged wood trim, sill plate, or fascia board
Inspect property for entry points, droppings, nesting, and activity patterns
Set snap traps and secured bait stations in activity areas
Tamper-resistant rodent bait stations placed around building perimeter
Seal gaps, cracks, pipe penetrations, and vents with steel wool, caulk, and metal flashing
Return visits to check traps, replenish bait, and monitor activity
Remove contaminated insulation and droppings, sanitize attic space
Thorough inspection of beds, furniture, baseboards, and crevices for bed bug activity
Raise room temperature above 120 degrees for 4-6 hours to kill all life stages
Targeted residual spray and dust application to cracks, crevices, and harborage areas
Bed bug proof encasements to trap and prevent bed bug harborage
Climb-up interceptor traps placed under bed legs for monitoring
Return visit to inspect and re-treat if needed, check for remaining activity
Identify species, locate entry points, assess damage, and develop removal plan
Set humane live traps, monitor, and relocate captured wildlife
Seal all entry points with metal flashing, hardware cloth, or foam after removal
Remove nesting material, droppings, and contaminated insulation
Enzyme-based sanitizer and deodorizer for affected areas
Return visit to confirm no re-entry and check all sealed areas

terminologyGlossary.IPM: Integrated Pest Management - a systematic approach using inspection, identification, and least-toxic treatments
terminologyGlossary.liquid barrier: A chemical perimeter treatment applied to soil or foundation to prevent pest entry
terminologyGlossary.bait station: A secured container holding poisoned bait for target pests like ants, roaches, or rodents
terminologyGlossary.gel bait: A ready-to-use bait applied in small dots in cracks and crevices for roaches and ants
terminologyGlossary.residual spray: A pesticide that remains active on treated surfaces for weeks or months after application
terminologyGlossary.non-repellent: A termiticide that pests cannot detect, allowing them to walk through it and transfer to colony
terminologyGlossary.exclusion: Physical methods of sealing entry points to prevent pest access to a structure
terminologyGlossary.harborage: A sheltered area where pests hide, nest, and reproduce
terminologyGlossary.conducive condition: Property conditions that attract or support pest activity, such as moisture or wood-soil contact
terminologyGlossary.WDI report: Wood-Destroying Insect report, often required for real estate transactions
terminologyGlossary.fumigation: Sealing a structure and introducing gas to kill all pests inside, primarily for drywood termites
terminologyGlossary.re-entry interval: The time period after pesticide application before people or pets can safely re-enter the area
terminologyGlossary.colony elimination: The goal of termite bait systems - workers carry bait back to the colony to destroy it
terminologyGlossary.dust application: Insecticidal dust blown into wall voids, attics, and other concealed spaces

seasonalTips.tip: Spring brings ant season and termite swarms. Push termite inspections and general pest initial treatments. Great time for new recurring service signups.
Peak pest season for ants, roaches, mosquitoes, and wasps. Ensure trucks are fully stocked and schedules are tight.
Rodents start seeking shelter as temperatures drop. Market exclusion work and rodent prevention services proactively.
Indoor pest pressure increases as pests overwinter inside homes. Focus on recurring service renewals and spider treatments.

followUpToneNotes: Pest control customers want reassurance that their home is safe and the problem will be resolved. Be confident but not alarmist. Explain the treatment process and what to expect. Emphasize the value of ongoing prevention versus one-time reactive treatments. Discuss product safety clearly, especially regarding children and pets. Always recommend an ongoing maintenance plan.
